title: Digital Assets and Estate Planning
description: Identify types of digital assets requiring estate planning, apply RUFADAA rules, create strategies for digital asset transfer and access.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Digital assets are increasingly valuable parts of estates. Understanding how to plan for them is essential for modern estate planning. From cryptocurrency to social media accounts, digital assets present unique challenges.

sections.title: Types of Digital Assets
rows: Cryptocurrency, online banking, payment apps (Venmo, PayPal)
Websites, domains, online stores, digital intellectual property
Game accounts, in-game purchases, virtual items

sections.title: RUFADAA Legal Framework
sections.content:
The Revised Uniform Fiduciary Access to Digital Assets Act grants fiduciaries access to decedents' digital accounts. Most states have adopted it with variations.

Key principle: Terms-of-service controls, unless user directs otherwise.

sections.title: Hierarchy of Authority
sections.content:
1. User's online tool (platform-specific settings)
2. User's estate plan (will, trust, power of attorney)
3. Terms of service (default if no instructions)

sections.title: Platform Legacy Tools
rows: Nothing built-in; rely on estate documents

sections.title: Cryptocurrency Challenges
rows: If lost, assets are permanently inaccessible
Trade-off between protection and fiduciary access

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: The PRIMARY risk with cryptocurrency in estates is loss of private keys making assets inaccessible. Without private keys or seed phrases, cryptocurrency cannot be accessed and is effectively lost forever.

sections.title: Cryptocurrency Planning Strategies
items: Hardware wallet with documented seed phrase: Secure, but phrase must be accessible
Multi-signature wallet: Multiple parties needed to access
Custodial solutions: Third-party holds keys (Coinbase, etc.)
Digital asset trust: Specialized trust with crypto provisions

sections.title: Creating a Digital Estate Plan
sections.content:
Step 1: Inventory Digital Assets
Document account names, URLs, usernames, access methods, and estimated values.

Step 2: Update Estate Documents
Include digital asset provisions in will, trust, power of attorney, and separate memorandum (updated regularly).

Step 3: Set Up Platform Tools
Configure each platform's legacy settings when available.

Step 4: Secure Access Information
Options: Password manager with shared vault; letter to executor with passwords; digital asset vault service; split knowledge (attorney + family).

sections.title: Sample Will Language
sections.content: "I authorize my Executor to access, manage, distribute, and delete any of my digital assets, accounts, and electronically stored information, including but not limited to email accounts, social media, cryptocurrency, and cloud storage, to the extent permitted by law and applicable terms of service."

sections.title: Email and Social Media Policies
rows: Inactive Account Manager; may provide data with court order
Memorialize or delete; Legacy Contact manages

sections.title: Summary
items: Inventory digital assets regularly and keep list updated
Use platform tools to designate legacy contacts
Include digital asset provisions in will, trust, and POA
Document cryptocurrency access securely but accessibly
Store access information where fiduciaries can find it

knowledgeCheck.question: Under the Revised Uniform Fiduciary Access to Digital Assets Act (RUFADAA), which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding a fiduciary's access to a decedent's digital assets?
options: A fiduciary has unrestricted access to all of the decedent's digital assets, regardless of the terms-of-service agreement.
A fiduciary's access is primarily governed by the online service's terms-of-service agreement, unless the user provided explicit directions otherwise in a will, trust, or other record.
A fiduciary can only access digital assets if explicitly named in the decedent's will as the digital asset executor.
RUFADAA grants access only to email accounts, not other types of digital assets.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: RUFADAA prioritizes the terms-of-service agreement of the online service provider unless the user has provided directions otherwise. This is a core principle of the act.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the balance between user privacy and fiduciary responsibility under RUFADAA.

reveal.question: List three strategies that can be used to transfer digital assets as part of an estate plan.
reveal.answer: 1. **Will or Trust Instructions:** Explicitly outlining how specific digital assets should be handled and who should receive them. 2. **Digital Asset Inventory:** Creating a detailed inventory of all digital assets, including usernames, passwords, and recovery information, stored securely. 3. **Durable Power of Attorney:** Granting a trusted individual the authority to manage digital assets if the owner becomes incapacitated.

rows.label: Monetary Value
values: Direct Monetary Value (Exchangeable)
Indirect Monetary Value (Brand/Influence)

rows.label: Transferability
values: Transferable via blockchain or exchange
Transferable, but often subject to platform policies

rows.label: Privacy Concerns
values: High privacy concerns; requires secure storage and access
Varying privacy concerns; dependent on platform settings and content

title: Trust Administration and Fiduciary Duties
description: Explain fiduciary duties of trustees and executors, apply prudent investor rules, manage trust distributions and documentation.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Serving as a trustee or executor carries significant legal responsibilities. Understanding fiduciary duties and administration requirements is essential for CFP professionals advising clients who serve in these roles.

sections.title: What Is a Fiduciary?
sections.content: A person obligated to act in the best interest of another. This creates a heightened standard of care and loyalty.

sections.title: Key Fiduciary Duties
rows: Balance interests of current and remainder beneficiaries
Invest and manage with care, skill, and caution
Provide regular accountings of trust activity
Carry out trust terms and applicable law

sections.title: Consequences of Breach
rows: In cases of theft or fraud

sections.title: The Prudent Investor Rule
sections.content:
Modern Portfolio Theory Standard:
• Diversification required unless imprudent under circumstances
• Risk/return balance considering portfolio as a whole
• Delegation permitted to investment advisors
• Consider all circumstances: beneficiary needs, time horizon, tax impact

sections.title: Investment Policy Statement Components
items: Objectives: Income needs, growth, liquidity
Time horizon: Trust duration, beneficiary ages
Risk tolerance: Based on beneficiary circumstances
Asset allocation: Target percentages by asset class
Rebalancing: When and how to adjust

sections.title: Trust Distribution Standards
rows: Health, Education, Maintenance, Support — Guided discretion

sections.title: HEMS Standard
sections.content: Health, Education, Maintenance, Support. The HEMS standard makes distributions excludable from beneficiary's taxable estate. It provides guided discretion for trustees.

sections.title: Documentation of Distributions
items: Distribution request: Beneficiary asks in writing
Trustee analysis: How request fits trust terms
Distribution memo: Decision and rationale
Beneficiary acknowledgment: Receipt of funds

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content:
Common Trust Administration Mistakes:
• Investment errors: Failing to diversify, keeping unsuitable inherited assets, self-dealing
• Administrative errors: Missing tax filings, co-mingling funds, unauthorized distributions
• Beneficiary relations: Favoritism, ignoring communications, failure to provide accountings

sections.title: Summary
items: Fiduciary duties include loyalty, prudence, impartiality, and accounting
Prudent investor rule focuses on total portfolio, permits delegation
Principal vs. income allocation affects different beneficiaries
HEMS standard provides guided discretion for distributions
Documentation is essential protection against liability

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following actions would MOST likely violate the Prudent Investor Rule, assuming no specific provisions in the trust document override this rule?
options: Delegating investment management to a qualified investment advisor.
Investing the trust assets primarily in a single, high-growth stock to maximize potential returns for the beneficiary.
Diversifying the trust portfolio across various asset classes, considering the beneficiary's needs and time horizon.
Regularly reviewing and adjusting the investment strategy based on market conditions and the beneficiary's evolving circumstances.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Investing primarily in a single stock violates the Prudent Investor Rule's requirement for diversification. The rule mandates diversification unless it is demonstrably imprudent under the specific circumstances. Concentrating assets in one stock exposes the portfolio to undue risk.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core principles of the Prudent Investor Rule - risk management and beneficiary needs.

reveal.question: Name at least three key fiduciary duties a trustee owes to the beneficiaries of a trust.
reveal.answer: Duties include: Duty of Loyalty (acting solely in the beneficiaries' best interest), Duty of Impartiality (treating all beneficiaries fairly), Duty of Prudence (managing assets with reasonable care, skill, and caution), Duty to Inform and Account (keeping beneficiaries informed about trust administration), and Duty to Control and Protect Trust Property.

rows.label: Governing Document
values: Trust Agreement
Will

rows.label: Asset Control
values: Controls assets already within the trust
Gathers and manages assets of the estate before distribution

rows.label: Duration of Role
values: Can be long-term, lasting for years or decades
Typically shorter-term, until estate is settled

rows.label: Oversight
values: May have ongoing court oversight depending on the trust type and jurisdiction
Typically requires court supervision and approval of actions.

title: International and Cross-Border Estate Planning
description: Identify issues with U.S. citizens and foreign assets, apply rules for non-citizen spouses, and analyze foreign trust reporting requirements.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Global mobility creates complex estate planning challenges. Understanding cross-border issues helps advisors serve clients with international connections.

sections.title: Non-Citizen Spouse Problem
sections.content: Unlimited marital deduction requires spouse to be a U.S. citizen. Non-citizen spouses are limited to the enhanced annual exclusion (~$190,000/year for gifts).

sections.title: QDOT Requirements
rows: U.S. bank as trustee, or post bond
Deferred until distributions to surviving spouse
Remaining assets taxed in survivor's estate

sections.title: QDOT Mechanics
sections.content:
1. Assets pass to QDOT at first spouse's death
2. Marital deduction allowed
3. Principal distributions trigger estate tax (as if first spouse's estate)
4. Income can be distributed without estate tax
5. Upon survivor's death, remaining principal taxed

sections.title: NRA Planning Strategies
items: Treaty benefits: Some increase exemption (e.g., Germany)
Holding structures: Foreign corporation owning U.S. real estate
Life insurance: Proceeds are not U.S.-situs
Joint ownership: Different rules for non-citizens

sections.title: Foreign Trust Definition
sections.content:
A foreign trust is one that:
• Has no U.S. trustee, or
• Is not under U.S. court supervision

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content:
Severe Penalties for Non-Compliance:
• Failure to file Form 3520: 35% of gross value of property
• Failure to file FBAR: Up to $100,000+ or 50% of account

Throwback rules may apply interest charges on deferred tax for accumulated income distributions.

sections.title: Multiple Probates
sections.content:
Each country where real estate is located usually requires probate. Strategies to avoid foreign probate:
• Revocable trust (may work in some jurisdictions)
• Joint ownership (depends on local law)
• Beneficiary designations (life insurance, retirement accounts)
• Local counsel is essential for proper planning

sections.title: Summary
items: Non-citizen spouses need QDOT for marital deduction
Non-resident aliens face limited exemption on U.S. assets (~$60K)
Foreign trusts trigger significant reporting requirements
Estate tax treaties may provide relief (check country-specific)
Multi-jurisdiction estates require coordination with local counsel

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold, a U.S. citizen, is married to Ingrid, a non-citizen. Harold dies, leaving his entire estate to a QDOT for Ingrid's benefit. Which of the following distributions from the QDOT will trigger U.S. estate tax?
options: Distributions of income to Ingrid for her health and maintenance.
Distributions of principal to Ingrid for her health and maintenance.
Distributions to Harold's children after Ingrid's death.
Distributions to pay for the administration expenses of the QDOT.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Distributions of principal from a QDOT to the non-citizen surviving spouse generally trigger U.S. estate tax, as if the distribution were part of the deceased spouse's estate. Income distributions are generally exempt. Distributions after the surviving spouse's death are also taxable, as the QDOT is treated as part of the deceased spouse's estate. Administration expenses are typically paid from the QDOT without triggering estate tax.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what the QDOT is designed to do – ensure estate tax is paid before the assets leave U.S. taxation.

reveal.question: What is the general purpose of U.S. foreign trust reporting requirements?
reveal.answer: The U.S. foreign trust reporting requirements aim to ensure U.S. tax compliance and prevent tax avoidance through the use of foreign trusts. These requirements mandate the disclosure of information about the trust's structure, assets, and beneficiaries to the IRS. This helps the IRS monitor and tax income and distributions related to the trust that are subject to U.S. taxation.

knowledgeCheck.question: John, a U.S. citizen, resides in the United States but owns a vacation home in France. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the U.S. estate tax implications of this foreign asset?
options: The vacation home is exempt from U.S. estate tax because it is located outside of the United States.
The vacation home is included in John's gross estate for U.S. estate tax purposes, regardless of its location.
The vacation home is only subject to U.S. estate tax if John dies while physically present in France.
The vacation home is subject to French estate tax, but not U.S. estate tax.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: U.S. citizens are subject to U.S. estate tax on their worldwide assets, regardless of where those assets are located. Therefore, the vacation home in France is included in John's gross estate for U.S. estate tax purposes. While French estate tax may also apply (depending on French law and any applicable treaty), it does not negate the U.S. estate tax liability.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the concept of worldwide taxation for U.S. citizens and residents.

title: Post-Mortem Estate Planning
description: Apply disclaimer strategies for tax optimization, evaluate post-mortem elections and choices, and analyze estate income tax planning.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Estate planning doesn't end at death. Post-mortem decisions can significantly affect family wealth. Understanding these strategies helps optimize outcomes after a client passes.

sections.title: What Is a Disclaimer?
sections.content: An irrevocable, unqualified refusal to accept property. Disclaimers allow flexibility in estate distribution even after death.

sections.title: Qualified Disclaimer Requirements
rows: Within 9 months of death or transfer

sections.title: Why Disclaim?
rows: Assets pass away from Medicaid applicant

sections.title: Disclaimer Example
sections.content:
Scenario: Husband's will leaves "All to wife"; wife's estate is already taxable.

Without disclaimer: Combined estates taxable.

With disclaimer: Wife disclaims enough to use husband's exemption; disclaimed portion passes to trust for wife's benefit. Result: Tax savings through use of both exemptions.

sections.title: Partial Disclaimers
sections.content: Partial disclaimers are allowed—disclaim a portion, accept the rest (if clearly severable). This provides flexibility in tax planning.

sections.title: Estate Income Tax Planning
sections.content:
The estate as a taxpayer hits the top 37% bracket at only ~$15,200 (2026), making tax planning critical.

Distributable Net Income (DNI): Income available for distribution. Estate deducts DNI distributed; beneficiary includes distributed DNI in their income.

Strategy: Distribute income to lower-bracket beneficiaries. If estate keeps income: taxed at 37%. If estate distributes: taxed at beneficiary's (probably lower) rate.

sections.title: 65-Day Election
sections.content: Distributions made within 65 days after year-end can be treated as made in prior tax year. Use case: Year 1 has high estate income; make distribution in January; elect to treat as Year 1 distribution; estate gets deduction in Year 1.

sections.title: Key Executor Elections
rows: Reduced value for farm/business real estate

sections.title: Alternate Valuation Date
sections.content:
When available: Only if reduces gross estate AND tax.
Valuation date: 6 months after death.
If sold within 6 months: Use sale date value.

Stepped basis reflects chosen valuation date.

sections.title: Exam Warning
sections.content: Portability Election: If you fail to file Form 706, deceased spouse's unused exemption is lost forever. You must file Form 706 even if no estate tax is due to preserve portability. May qualify for 2-year safe harbor if filed late.

sections.title: Estate Settlement Timeline
rows: Notify beneficiaries, file will with court

sections.title: Date of Death vs. Alternate Valuation
Summary
items: Disclaimers can optimize tax outcomes even after death
65-day election allows income shifting to prior year
Portability election preserves unused exemption—must file Form 706
Alternate valuation available only if it reduces estate and tax
Estate income tax rates are steep—distribute income when possible

knowledgeCheck.question: Harold dies, leaving his entire estate to his wife, Wendy. Wendy's estate is already substantial and projected to exceed the estate tax exemption amount. To minimize estate taxes, what is the MOST appropriate course of action Wendy should consider?
options: Wendy should accept all assets from Harold's estate to maximize her control over the combined assets.
Wendy should disclaim a portion of the assets, allowing them to pass to a credit shelter trust for her benefit, utilizing Harold's estate tax exemption.
Wendy should immediately gift a portion of the inherited assets to her children to reduce the size of her estate.
Wendy should petition the court to redirect Harold's assets to a charitable organization.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: By disclaiming a portion of the assets, Wendy allows those assets to pass into a credit shelter trust (or similar arrangement) that utilizes Harold's unused estate tax exemption. This shelters those assets from estate tax in Wendy's estate while still potentially providing her with benefits from the trust. The other options are less effective for estate tax minimization in this scenario.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the primary goal of using both spouses' estate tax exemptions.

reveal.question: Name at least three common post-mortem elections an executor might make to manage the estate effectively.
reveal.answer:
Possible answers include: 
1. Alternate Valuation Date Election (IRC Section 2032)
2. Qualified Terminable Interest Property (QTIP) Election (IRC Section 2056(b)(7))
3. Deduction for Administration Expenses on Estate Tax Return (Form 706) or Fiduciary Income Tax Return (Form 1041)
4. Disclaimer Decisions

sections.title: Estate Income Tax vs. Estate Tax

rows.label: Tax Base
values: Income earned by the estate after death
Value of assets in the estate at date of death (or alternate valuation date)

rows.label: Filing Requirement
values: Required if estate has gross income exceeding a certain threshold (relatively low)
Required if gross estate exceeds the estate tax exemption amount

rows.label: Deductions
values: Ordinary and necessary business expenses, distributions to beneficiaries
Funeral expenses, debts, administrative expenses, charitable bequests, marital deduction

rows.label: Tax Rates
values: Individual income tax rates
Progressive estate tax rates
